episode_title: Natali Helberger: Audience-Centric Approaches to AI Ethics in Media

codes.description: Nearly 20% of surveyed newsrooms adopting complete AI avoidance as their primary ethical strategy, reflecting both genuine concern and innovation paralysis
excerpts: the top response, which was nearly 20% of the court, said that this strategy was simply not to use AI because of that ethical concerns
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.code: AI as cultural problem not just technical
codes.description: Reframing AI implementation challenges from purely technical to fundamentally ethical and cultural, requiring value alignment alongside system integration
excerpts: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The paradox that the most commonly cited ethical safeguard (human oversight of output) cannot detect many critical issues like training data bias that are invisible in the output
excerpts: there is this very strong belief which we also see reflected in other research that as long there's a human watching over AI, it is more trustworthy, which is something that really requires some unpacking because we can of course observe the output. But part of what makes AI and the use of AI responsible or not is also the input and the model
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The finding that auditing and responsible procurement of AI systems was the least popular ethical strategy despite being one of the most effective -- a critical blind spot
excerpts: Another interesting thing in the survey was that least popular or least most mostly used strategy was to actually order the systems or do responsible procurement and look into What kind of tools are we working with?
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The second most pressing ethical concern being output problems: inaccuracy, bias, hallucination, and quality deficits in AI-generated content
excerpts: The second most pressing concern we saw is problems with the output, inaccurate information, biases, lack of quality in the content
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The surprising absence of environmental and ecological concerns from newsroom AI ethics discussions, despite these being prominent in public discourse
excerpts: when we talk at AI in a public discourse we talk of for example also very much about ecological concerns and the role of big tech companies. And I found it very interesting that these concerns were not mentioned so much or almost not mentioned in the survey
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Newsrooms still conceiving of AI as an inert tool (like a typewriter or computer) rather than as part of a broader societal transformation
excerpts: AI at the moment is still seen as a tool, like a computer or mobile phone or a typewriter and less as a part of a transformation of the way we do media and of society
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The argument that 'human-in-the-loop' oversight should extend beyond editors and journalists to include audience members who can provide feedback, flag errors, and complain
excerpts: Someone who's a party here that is at the moment completely excluded from the loop are, for example, users. And their ability to feedback or to criticize or to let the media organization know when something goes wrong. And in our research we actually saw that users would very much appreciate being included in this loop
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: A concrete tool developed from research and practitioner interviews listing critical questions about data quality, security, liability, explainability, support access, pricing, and ecological footprint for AI procurement
excerpts: based on these conversations and our own research we made a first draft for a procurement checklist
What happens to the data that is fed in? Is that secured? Will that be used for training... But also questions about liability. Whose liability is it when something goes wrong?
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: AI providers shifting liability for failures onto professional end users (journalists, newsrooms) through terms of service, creating unexamined legal exposure
excerpts: if you look in the terms of use of a lot of these companies you will find that most of the liability is dumped on the end user, the professional user. So, for example, a journalist using or media organization using the technology
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Smaller newsrooms lacking legal departments and unable to effectively evaluate AI vendors or reach them when problems arise
excerpts: especially for smaller newsrooms who do not have a major legal department who simply find it very difficult what to look out for when procuring technology
it's really difficult or if not impossible to reach these large providers, especially if you are working at a smaller newsroom
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The critical gap between writing ethical AI guidelines (a pledge on paper) and actually internalizing them into working practices, skills, routines, funding, and incentives
excerpts: Responsible use of AI is not simply something that you can write up in a guideline and that it will automatically materialize, but one also really needs to think of how that should be then internalized in working practices, in skills, in new routines and also in times, funds and incentives
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Observation that newsroom AI guidelines globally are converging toward shared principles (transparency, human oversight, data privacy, source protection), reducing the need to start from scratch
excerpts: There has been also research comparing these guidelines and finding that they are more and more converging to certain shared principles. For example, the importance of transparency of human oversight, but also respect for data privacy or source protection
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Unpacking 'transparency' into multiple distinct questions: transparency for whom (user, journalist, editor), about what, provided by whom (R&D, vendor), and in what form to be meaningful
excerpts: transparency for whom? So transparency for the end user, but maybe also transparency for the journalist, for the editor who has to work with the tools? Is that there? What do these different parties need to know in order to be able to assess automated content upon its value?
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: A disconnect between the theoretical commitment to making AI models explainable and the practical challenge of making that explainability actionable and meaningful for users and editors
excerpts: There's a lot of talk about explainability, making these models explainable. There's less talk on how to make explainability in a way that is actually actionable and really meaningful
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Research finding less empirical evidence for filter bubbles in Europe than feared, partly because people use multiple outlets in a healthy media ecosystem
excerpts: actually there was less empirical evidence for these filter bubbles, at least in Europe, really materializing to that extent. And part of that is that people do not simply use one outlet
as long as we have a healthy media ecosystem with a diversity of voices and outlets, and people are actually using those. This fear of filter bubbles is less pressing
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The filter bubble and polarization risk is concentrated among users who receive news exclusively through a single platform like TikTok or Instagram, where commercial engagement incentives dominate
excerpts: It is pressing for those people who only receive their news via TikTok or only via Instagram because then you have a highly curated view of your reality and especially on social media platforms the commercial incentives to how to optimize your algorithm are very much driven by considerations of engagement, time spent, clicks
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The positive potential of recommendation algorithms to proactively diversify content exposure, introducing users to voices and perspectives they would not have discovered otherwise
excerpts: you can use diversity recommenders also to diversify choices and recommend people content that they not have discovered yet or might maybe be interested in but do not know about or would like to have more in-depth information
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: Three specific metrics developed for evaluating news recommender diversity: fragmentation (overlap between users), activation (inspiring participation), and representation (balance of perspectives)
excerpts: the level of fragmentation. If you take all recommendations in an aggregate, to what extent is the overlap between stories shown to different users
activation... to what extent the issued recommendation is aimed at inspiring a user to take action
representation to what extent different recommendations provide the balance of different opinions and perspectives
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The research finding that transparency about algorithmic parameters alone does not increase trust; it only becomes meaningful when paired with user agency to make choices
excerpts: being transparent alone does not necessarily increase trust of people but they want also to have agency

Being transparent about what parameter you use is very nice and good, but it starts only being really meaningful if you also give people a choice maybe between different parameters or recommendation logics
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: User concern about being locked into outdated algorithmic profiles that no longer represent their interests, highlighting the need for periodic profile review mechanisms
excerpts: remind them from time to time about their profile and if that still represents what they're really interested in Because that is something that we also saw that a lot of people are very concerned about, you know, being labeled as this Early morning person who only drinks latte and listens to Taylor Swift on Spotify
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The argument that the design of how user choices are presented (easy and intuitive vs. buried in settings) determines whether agency is real or theoretical
excerpts: if you have to dive deep down into the system and then click five times and find then somewhere in the small print these one button that you need to press, of course nobody's going to exercise this choice. But if you make it easy and intuitive, then I think the likelihood of people exercising this choice is immediately much more increased
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The EU AI Act's structure of categorizing AI applications by risk level and applying regulations proportionally, attempting to balance innovation enablement with rights protection
excerpts: It's a risk-based approach, which means it really focuses on a bit the minimum standard. So where is AI really hurting us and what to do about that
tries to strike a balance between on the one hand enabling innovation and not killing innovation by overregulation and on the other hand defining guardrails
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The EU AI Act's classification of journalistic AI use as outside the high-risk category, leaving media largely self-regulated with minimal transparency obligations
excerpts: The use of AI in the media is not on the list of high-risk AI. So that means journalistic AI is minimally regulated. Essentially, you have to be transparent if you use AI to create pictures and text. Unless you are a media organization under editorial oversight, then even this obligation doesn't apply to you
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The fundamental limitation that AI transparency labels and regulations only constrain responsible actors while bad-faith actors producing deepfakes will simply not comply
excerpts: it's very much a regulation of the willing. So if you produce text and you are a responsible journalist and you label your content, that's very nice. But if you produce a deepfake to derail an election or dissuade people from voting, will you use a label to show people that your deepfake has been faked? Probably not
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The argument that simple 'made with AI' labels are both inadequate (do not distinguish quality AI-assisted content from deceptive content) and soon to be ubiquitous to the point of meaninglessness
excerpts: Simply the fact that something has been made with AI does not necessarily say that this is bad content. So I think it's a good first step. But I also think it doesn't tell you enough
very soon we all will have generative AI on our laptops and computers... So simply the fact that something has been made with AI does not necessarily say that this is bad content
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The concern that proliferating AI labels across platforms will lead to user fatigue and eventual inattention to these disclosures
excerpts: users will be more and more exposed to all kinds of labels which probably at some point also leads to label blindness and they say simply don't look
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.code: news media vs. social media differentiation opportunity
codes.description: Responsible AI use (fact-checking, human oversight, procurement, transparency) as a competitive differentiator for news media against social media platforms
excerpts: I think we really need to think of how to distinguish the media and make sure that people know if I want trustworthy content, I go there because it is fact-checked, there's human oversight, someone has been testing the system, there was responsible procurement, they are transparent about it
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The alarming research finding that user trust in news media's responsible AI use is in some countries comparable to their trust in social media platforms, eroding a key competitive advantage
excerpts: users trust that the news media use AI and algorithms responsibly is a bit in some countries on par with their trust in social media using that responsibly
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: A proposed principle that AI transparency disclosure is needed only when AI has significant journalistic impact (content generation, audience communication) but not for backend tools (translation, transcription)
excerpts: it's important to be transparent about the applications of AI where it really matters for the audience and the audience is concerned
The threshold is really reached once you getting closer to core journalistic functions like producing and informing the audience and communicating with the audience
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The striking absence of direct audience input in determining what AI transparency information they actually need, despite these decisions being made on their behalf
excerpts: something that really strikes me is how little we ask users about what kind of information they need. Because in the end they have to judge that, right?
next week we do focus groups with users here in Amsterdam to actually ask them what would you like to know and what would help you to decide whether or not to trust this content
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The quantitative finding that 60% of Dutch respondents would feel misled by undisclosed AI-generated content, establishing a strong empirical basis for transparency obligations
excerpts: 60% of our respondents here in the Netherlands said that they would feel misled if something was generated by AI and they wouldn't be informed about it
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The Nordic AI initiative's recommendation to be specific about which AI tool was used and for what purpose rather than generic 'made with AI' labels, and to harmonize industry terminology
excerpts: they say AI with significant journalistic impact requires transparency. Another thing they suggest is be very specific about the type of AI tool applied, so not simply made with AI, but made with which tool and to what end but also harmonize the industry's language around generative AI
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The argument that deeper questions about authenticity, provenance, and the commitment of the creating entity to quality are more important than simple AI/not-AI binary labels
excerpts: I think we really need to ask deeper question about what is the epistemological truth or what's why is it important to know whether something has been AI made or not And also maybe equally important is who has made it and is the entity that made it do they commit to critical checking
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.code: willingness to pay for quality personalization
codes.description: An academic AI ethics expert expressing personal willingness to pay for a well-designed personalized news recommendation system -- signaling unmet market demand
excerpts: if there is a media organization who would manage to develop a really good personalized recommendation system that knows when I need which kind of content, I probably would be even willing to pay for that
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: An AI ethics researcher personally refusing to use home voice assistants despite being professionally immersed in AI, citing privacy concerns
excerpts: I don't have an echo or Alexa or something like that because I really worry about my privacy. So this is not getting into my room
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

codes.description: The generative AI boom serving as a positive forcing function that compelled newsrooms to critically review and update their long-dormant editorial guidelines
excerpts: these journalistic guidelines for a very long time they were somewhere on the website of organization and not really updated. Many of them didn't even mention the word digital until last year. And something that the whole Gen AI boom really achieved was critical thinking about these guidelines
codes.speaker: Natali Helberger

episode_summary: This episode reframes the AI ethics conversation in journalism from a producer-centric concern to an audience-centric one, arguing that the most consequential gap in current approaches is the systematic exclusion of users from the 'human-in-the-loop' paradigm they are supposed to protect. Helberger's research reveals a paradox at the heart of newsroom AI strategy: the most popular ethical safeguard (human oversight of output) is structurally incapable of catching the most dangerous problems (training data bias, model architecture flaws), while the most effective safeguard (responsible procurement and auditing) is the least practiced. The conversation introduces a sophisticated challenge to transparency orthodoxy, demonstrating that transparency without agency is functionally meaningless, and proposes concrete alternatives through diversity recommender metrics (fragmentation, activation, representation) and choice architecture design. Perhaps most provocatively, the episode surfaces the 'regulation of the willing' problem -- that AI labeling requirements and ethical guidelines only bind responsible actors while bad-faith producers of election deepfakes will simply ignore them -- calling into question the entire edifice of voluntary AI ethics frameworks.
surprises: The least popular ethical strategy among newsrooms was responsible procurement and system auditing -- the approach most likely to catch upstream problems like biased training data that cannot be detected by examining output alone.

Ecological concerns about AI energy use were almost entirely absent from newsroom survey responses despite being a major theme in public discourse, suggesting newsrooms conceptualize AI as an inert tool rather than a resource-intensive infrastructure.

Filter bubble fears in Europe appear empirically overblown because multi-outlet media consumption provides a natural counterweight, shifting the concern specifically to single-platform users (TikTok-only, Instagram-only).

A leading AI ethics professor who spends her career studying responsible AI still refuses to have an Alexa in her home, but would willingly pay for a good news recommendation system -- revealing a highly specific personal risk calculus.

User trust in news media's responsible AI use is in some countries on par with trust in social media companies, suggesting the assumed trust advantage of news organizations is far more fragile than the industry assumes.
tensions: Between human oversight as the most popular ethical safeguard and its structural inability to detect training data bias, model architecture problems, and other upstream issues.
Between the desire for transparency about AI use and the risk of 'label blindness' as AI labels proliferate across every platform and product.
Between the EU AI Act's risk-based regulatory approach and the fact that journalistic AI use falls outside high-risk categories, effectively leaving media self-regulated.
Between the aspiration for responsible AI guidelines and the persistent gap between written principles and actual internalization into working practices, skills, funding, and incentives.

Between the ideal of giving users agency over recommendation algorithms and the counter-argument that users will not exercise choice -- resolved by Helberger through the argument that choice architecture design determines uptake.
